does: This paper analyzes how preference optimization methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) cause language models to rely on spurious correlations, leading to problems like sycophancy and length bias. The authors provide a unified theoretical framework for log-linear policies that identifies two mechanisms driving spurious learning: mean spurious bias and causal-spurious correlation leakage. They characterize how this reliance creates irreducible vulnerabilities to distribution shift, where additional training data from the same distribution cannot reduce dependence on spurious features. The paper then proposes tie training, a data augmentation strategy using equal-utility preference pairs that introduces data-driven regularization to selectively reduce spurious feature reliance while maintaining performance on genuine features.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about preference learning methods in large language models, responding to documented issues like sycophancy and length bias observed in systems trained with DPO and similar approaches. It extends theoretical work on spurious correlation learning and causal feature reliance in machine learning, connecting to broader research on robustness to distribution shift and goal misgeneralization. The paper draws on mechanistic understanding of how neural networks learn features and relates to prior work analyzing preference learning objectives. It also connects to literature on data augmentation strategies for improving robustness and the theoretical study of log-linear policy optimization in reinforcement learning contexts.
enables: This work establishes a principled framework for understanding and mitigating spurious learning in preference optimization, opening directions for developing more robust preference learning methods. The tie training approach provides a concrete technique applicable to existing preference learning pipelines, enabling practitioners to reduce model sycophancy and bias issues. Theoretically, the analysis of distribution shift vulnerabilities motivates future research into alternative preference learning objectives that are inherently more robust to spurious correlations. The framework could enable better evaluation metrics for preference learning that directly measure reliance on spurious features. More broadly, this work points toward methods for ensuring that larger language models with advanced preference learning remain aligned with genuine objectives even under deployment distribution shifts.
